title: Python Programming: From Zero to Hero
description: A complete Python course covering basics, data structures, OOP, file handling, and databases — packed with hands-on programming challenges, MCQs, MSQs, and SQL exercises.

Section 1: Python Basics.description: Master the building blocks of Python: variables, arithmetic, input/output, and type conversion.

questions.question: Write a program that prints 'Hello, Python!' to the console.
questions.explanation: Use the built-in print() function to output text. String literals are enclosed in single or double quotes. print('Hello, Python!') sends that text to standard output.

questions.question: Read two integers from separate lines and print their sum.
questions.explanation: input() reads a line as a string. int() converts it to an integer. Add the two integers and print the result using print().

questions.question: Given length and width on separate lines, print the area of the rectangle.
questions.explanation: Area = length × width. Read both values with int(input()), multiply them, and print the product.

questions.question: Given a temperature in Celsius, convert and print it in Fahrenheit. Formula: F = C * 9/5 + 32. Print with 2 decimal places.
questions.explanation: Read the Celsius value as a float. Apply the formula F = C * 9/5 + 32. Use print(f'{F:.2f}') or round() to format to 2 decimal places.

questions.question: Read two integers a and b. Swap their values and print a then b on separate lines.
questions.explanation: Python supports simultaneous assignment: a, b = b, a swaps both in one step without a temporary variable. Then print a and b on separate lines.

questions.question: Given an integer N, print 'Even' if it is even, otherwise print 'Odd'.
questions.explanation: The modulo operator % gives the remainder of division. If N % 2 == 0 the number is even; otherwise it is odd.

questions.question: Given three integers on separate lines, print the largest.
questions.explanation: Python's built-in max() function accepts multiple arguments and returns the greatest. Alternatively use nested if-elif-else comparisons.

questions.question: Given an integer N (possibly negative), print its absolute value.
questions.explanation: Python's built-in abs() function returns the non-negative value of a number. For negative numbers it flips the sign; positive numbers are unchanged.

questions.question: Given base B and exponent E on separate lines, print B raised to the power E.
questions.explanation: Python's ** operator computes powers: B ** E. The built-in pow(B, E) is equivalent. Both handle large integers natively.

questions.question: Read two integers and an operator (+, -, *, //) on separate lines. Print the result of applying the operator. For division use integer (floor) division.
questions.explanation: Use if-elif to check the operator string. Apply the corresponding Python operator. // is floor division (integer result). Handle each case separately.

Section 2: Loops and Iteration.description: Explore for loops, while loops, range(), and common iteration patterns.

questions.question: Given N, print all integers from 1 to N inclusive, each on a new line.
questions.explanation: range(1, N+1) generates integers starting at 1 up to and including N. Iterate with a for loop and print each value.

questions.questionname: Sum of First N Natural Numbers
questions.question: Given N, print the sum of all natural numbers from 1 to N.
questions.explanation: The formula is N*(N+1)//2 for O(1) time. Alternatively accumulate with a loop: total = 0; for i in range(1, N+1): total += i.

questions.question: Given N, print N! (N factorial).
questions.explanation: Factorial: N! = 1 × 2 × ... × N. Use a loop with a running product starting at 1. Python handles arbitrarily large integers natively. 0! = 1 by definition.

questions.question: Given N, print the first N Fibonacci numbers separated by spaces. F(1)=1, F(2)=1, F(n)=F(n-1)+F(n-2).
questions.explanation: Start with a=1, b=1. Print a, then repeatedly update a,b = b, a+b and print until N numbers are printed. Collect in a list and join with spaces.

questions.question: Given N, print the multiplication table of N from 1 to 10. Format: 'N x i = result'.
questions.explanation: Loop i from 1 to 10 using range(1, 11). On each iteration print the formatted string using an f-string: f'{N} x {i} = {N*i}'.

questions.question: Given a positive integer N, print how many digits it has.
questions.explanation: Convert N to a string with str() and use len() to count characters. Alternatively divide by 10 in a while loop until N becomes 0, counting iterations.

questions.question: Given an integer N, print its digits in reverse order as an integer (no leading zeros).
questions.explanation: Convert to string, slice with [::-1] to reverse, then convert back to int to strip leading zeros. Print the integer.

questions.question: Given N, print 'YES' if it is prime, else print 'NO'.
questions.explanation: A prime number is only divisible by 1 and itself. Check divisors from 2 up to sqrt(N) using int(N**0.5)+1. If any divide N evenly it is not prime. Edge cases: N<2 is not prime.

questions.question: Given a positive integer N, print the sum of its digits.
questions.explanation: Convert N to a string, iterate over each character, convert back to int with int(ch), and accumulate the sum. Alternatively use repeated modulo: digit = N%10; N //= 10.

questions.question: Given N, print a right-angled triangle of stars with N rows. Row i has i stars.
questions.explanation: Use a for loop with i from 1 to N. On each iteration print '*' * i to produce i stars. The * operator repeats a string.

questions.question: Given two integers A and B, print their Greatest Common Divisor (GCD).
questions.explanation: Use the Euclidean algorithm: while B != 0: A, B = B, A % B. When B is 0, A holds the GCD. Python's math.gcd() does this in one call.

questions.question: Given N, print the count of prime numbers from 2 to N inclusive using the Sieve of Eratosthenes.
questions.explanation: Create a boolean list sieve of size N+1, all True. Set sieve[0]=sieve[1]=False. For each i from 2 to sqrt(N), if sieve[i] is True, mark multiples of i as False starting from i*i. Count remaining True values.

Section 3: Strings.description: Work with Python strings: slicing, methods, palindromes, anagrams, and formatting.

questions.question: Given a string, print it reversed.
questions.explanation: Python strings support slicing. s[::-1] returns the string with step -1, effectively reversing it. This is the most Pythonic approach.

questions.question: Given a string, print 'YES' if it is a palindrome, else print 'NO'. Case-insensitive.
questions.explanation: Convert to lowercase with s.lower(). Compare s to s[::-1]. A palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards (e.g., 'racecar', 'Level').

questions.question: Given a string, print the number of vowels (a, e, i, o, u — case-insensitive).
questions.explanation: Convert to lowercase. Iterate over each character and check if it is in the set {'a','e','i','o','u'}. Using a set gives O(1) lookup. Increment a counter for each vowel.

questions.question: Given a sentence, print the number of words in it.
questions.explanation: str.split() without arguments splits on any whitespace and removes empty strings. len() of the resulting list gives the word count.
Example:
Input: Hello World how are you
Output: 5

questions.question: Given two strings on separate lines, print 'YES' if they are anagrams of each other (case-insensitive), else 'NO'.
questions.explanation: Two strings are anagrams if they contain the same characters with the same frequencies. Sort both lowercased strings and compare. Or use collections.Counter to get frequency maps and compare those.

questions.question: Given a string and a shift K, apply Caesar cipher to all alphabetic characters (preserve case and non-alpha). Print the encoded string.
questions.explanation: For each character: if it's uppercase, shift within A-Z using chr((ord(c)-65+K)%26+65). For lowercase use 97. Non-alphabetic characters stay unchanged. The modulo 26 wraps around the alphabet.
Example:
Input: Hello, World!
3
Output: Khoor, Zruog!

questions.question: Given a sentence, print the longest word. If there is a tie, print the first one.
questions.explanation: Split the sentence into words with split(). Use max() with key=len to find the word with the greatest length. max() returns the first maximum in case of ties.
Example:
Input: The quick brown fox
Output: quick

questions.question: Given a sentence in lowercase, convert it to title case (first letter of every word capitalised) and print it.
questions.explanation: Python's str.title() converts to title case automatically. Alternatively use ' '.join(word.capitalize() for word in s.split()).
Example:
Input: hello world from python
Output: Hello World From Python

Section 4: Lists and Data Structures.description: Master Python lists, tuples, sets, dictionaries, and common algorithms.

questions.question: Read N integers separated by spaces on one line. Print the maximum value.
questions.explanation: Use list(map(int, input().split())) to parse the input into a list of integers. Then max() returns the largest element in O(n) time.

questions.question: Given N integers on one line, print them with duplicates removed, preserving original order.
questions.explanation: Use dict.fromkeys() or a seen set to track encountered values while iterating. dict.fromkeys(lst) preserves insertion order in Python 3.7+. Convert keys back to a list and print space-separated.

questions.question: Given a list of integers, print the second largest distinct value.
questions.explanation: Convert to a set to remove duplicates, sort in descending order, then take index 1. Alternatively sort the unique list and pick [-2]. Handle edge case where fewer than 2 distinct values exist (not tested here).

questions.question: Given N integers and a rotation value K on the next line, left-rotate the list by K positions and print the result space-separated.
questions.explanation: Left rotation by K means the first K elements move to the end. Slice: result = lst[K:] + lst[:K]. Use K % N to handle K >= N cases.

questions.question: Given N space-separated integers, print each unique value and its frequency, sorted by value ascending. Format: 'value: count'.
questions.explanation: Use collections.Counter(lst) to build a frequency dictionary. Sort the items by key. Print each key-value pair formatted as 'value: count'.

questions.question: Given N integers on the first line and a target T on the second line, print the 1-based indices of the two numbers that add up to T. Print the smaller index first.
questions.explanation: Use a dictionary mapping each value to its index. For each element, check if target - element is already in the dictionary. If yes, you found the pair. Use 1-based indexing in output.

questions.question: Given a 2D matrix (rows on separate lines, values space-separated), print all elements in row-major order, space-separated.
questions.explanation: Read each line, split and convert to integers, extend a result list. Alternatively use a list comprehension with nested loops. Join the final list with spaces for output.

questions.question: Given a string containing only '(', ')', '{', '}', '[', ']', print 'YES' if the brackets are balanced, else 'NO'.
questions.explanation: Use a stack (list in Python). For each opening bracket push it. For each closing bracket check if the stack is non-empty and the top matches. If any mismatch or stack not empty at end, it's unbalanced. Matching pairs: ()  {}  [].

Section 5: Functions and Recursion.description: Write clean reusable functions and master recursive thinking.

questions.question: Using recursion, compute N! and print it.
questions.explanation: Base case: factorial(0) = 1 or factorial(1) = 1. Recursive case: factorial(n) = n * factorial(n-1). Python handles the call stack for small N automatically.

questions.question: Using recursion with memoisation, print the N-th Fibonacci number (1-indexed, F(1)=1, F(2)=1).
questions.explanation: Naive recursion is exponential. Use @functools.lru_cache or a memo dict to cache results. fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2) with base cases fib(1)=fib(2)=1.

questions.question: Given a sorted list on the first line and a target T on the second line, print the 1-based index of T or -1 if not found.
questions.explanation: Binary search: maintain lo=0, hi=len-1. Compute mid=(lo+hi)//2. If arr[mid]==T return mid+1 (1-based). If arr[mid]<T, search right half (lo=mid+1); else search left (hi=mid-1). If lo>hi, return -1.

questions.question: Implement merge sort. Read N integers on one line, sort them in ascending order using merge sort, and print the sorted list space-separated.
questions.explanation: Divide: split the list into two halves. Conquer: recursively sort each half. Merge: combine two sorted halves by comparing elements one by one. Base case: list of length 0 or 1 is already sorted. Time complexity: O(n log n).

questions.question: Given base B and exponent E, compute B^E using fast exponentiation (recursion) and print it.
questions.explanation: Fast power: if E is even, B^E = (B^(E//2))^2. If odd, B^E = B * B^(E-1). This reduces the problem in half each step, giving O(log E) time. Base case: B^0 = 1.

Section 6: Object-Oriented Programming.description: Design Python classes, use inheritance, and implement OOP patterns.

questions.question: Implement a BankAccount class with deposit(amount) and withdraw(amount) methods. Read commands from input: 'D amount' for deposit, 'W amount' for withdraw. Print balance after each operation. Withdrawing more than balance should print 'Insufficient funds'.
questions.explanation: Define __init__ with self.balance = 0. deposit adds to balance. withdraw checks if amount <= balance before subtracting. Use a loop to read and process commands until EOF.
Example:
Input: D 100
W 30
W 200
Output: 100
70
Insufficient funds

questions.question: Create a base class Shape with an area() method. Create Circle (radius) and Rectangle (length, width) subclasses. Read shape type and dimensions, print area rounded to 2 decimal places. Use pi = 3.14159.
questions.explanation: Use inheritance: Circle and Rectangle extend Shape. Override area() in each. For Circle: area = pi * r^2. For Rectangle: area = l * w. isinstance() can identify the shape, or simply call area() polymorphically.

Section 7: File Handling & Comprehension.description: Work with list/dict comprehensions and simulate file-like data processing.

questions.question: Given N integers on one line, print the square of each, space-separated, using a list comprehension.
questions.explanation: List comprehension syntax: [expr for item in iterable]. Here: [x**2 for x in nums]. Then ' '.join(map(str, result)) to format output. This is more Pythonic and concise than a regular loop.

questions.question: Given N integers on one line, print only the even numbers in their original order, space-separated.
questions.explanation: Use a conditional comprehension: [x for x in nums if x % 2 == 0]. If result is empty print an empty line. This filters the list in a single readable expression.

questions.question: Given a sentence, create a dictionary of {word: len(word)} and print entries sorted alphabetically as 'word: length', one per line.
questions.explanation: Use a dict comprehension: {word: len(word) for word in sentence.split()}. Sort the dictionary keys with sorted() and iterate to print. This combines dict comprehension with sorted output.
Example:
Input: hello world python
Output: hello: 5
python: 6
world: 5

Section 8: Conceptual MCQ & MSQ.description: Test your Python theory knowledge with multiple choice and multi-select questions.

questions.question: Which of the following is an immutable data type in Python?
questions.explanation: Immutable types cannot be changed after creation. Tuples are immutable. Lists, sets, and dicts are mutable.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a list and a tuple in Python?
questions.explanation: Lists are mutable (can be changed), tuples are immutable (cannot be changed after creation). Both are ordered and allow duplicates.
options: Lists are ordered, tuples are not
Lists are mutable, tuples are immutable
Tuples allow duplicates, lists do not

questions.question: In Python's LEGB rule, what does 'E' stand for?
questions.explanation: LEGB = Local, Enclosing, Global, Built-in. This is the order Python searches for variable names. Enclosing refers to the scope of enclosing functions (closures).

questions.question: Which of the following correctly defines a lambda function that squares a number?
questions.explanation: Lambda syntax: lambda arguments: expression. lambda x: x**2 defines an anonymous function that returns x squared. Lambda functions are single-expression anonymous functions.

questions.question: What is the output of: def f(lst=[]): lst.append(1); return lst followed by print(f(), f())?
questions.explanation: Default mutable arguments are created once when the function is defined, not each call. So both calls share the same list object. f() → [1], f() → [1, 1]. This is a classic Python gotcha.

questions.question: Which of the following are built-in Python functions? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: len(), range(), and print() are all built-in Python functions available without any imports. shuffle() belongs to the random module and must be imported.

questions.question: Which of the following are core principles of Object-Oriented Programming?
questions.explanation: The four pillars of OOP are Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, and Polymorphism. Compilation and Recursion are programming concepts but not OOP principles.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid exception handling keywords in Python?
questions.explanation: try, except, finally, and else are all part of Python's exception handling mechanism. catch and throw are Java/C++ keywords and do not exist in Python.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of binary search expressed as the exponent X in O(log_2(N))? Enter the base of the logarithm.
questions.explanation: Binary search halves the search space at every step. After k steps, N/2^k = 1, so k = log_2(N). The base of the logarithm is 2, representing the halving at each comparison.

questions.question: In Python, if lst = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50], what is the value of lst[-1]?
questions.explanation: Negative indexing in Python counts from the end. lst[-1] is the last element, which is 50. lst[-2] would be 40, and so on.

questions.question: Which method returns all key-value pairs of a Python dictionary as tuples?
questions.explanation: dict.items() returns a view of (key, value) tuples. keys() returns just keys, values() returns just values, and pairs() does not exist in Python.

questions.question: What does lst[::2] return for lst = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]?
questions.explanation: Slice [::2] means start from 0, go to end, with step 2 — i.e., every other element. For [0,1,2,3,4,5] this gives [0, 2, 4].

Section 9: SQL for Python Developers.description: Practice SQL queries relevant to Python-backed applications — querying, aggregating, and joining tables.

questions.question: Write a SQL query to retrieve all records from the `users` table, ordered by `username` ascending.
questions.explanation: SELECT * retrieves all columns. ORDER BY username ASC sorts alphabetically. ASC is default but makes intent explicit.
questions.solution: SELECT * FROM users ORDER BY username ASC;

questions.question: Write a SQL query to return the `username` and `email` of all users older than 23, ordered by `age` descending.
questions.explanation: WHERE age > 23 filters rows. SELECT specific columns avoids retrieving unnecessary data. ORDER BY age DESC puts oldest users first.
questions.solution: SELECT username, email FROM users WHERE age > 23 ORDER BY age DESC;

questions.question: Write a SQL query to return the `category` and average `score` (aliased as `avg_score`, rounded to 2 decimal places) from the `quiz_results` table, grouped by category, ordered by `avg_score` descending.
questions.explanation: GROUP BY aggregates rows by category. AVG() computes the mean score. ROUND(..., 2) limits decimal places. ORDER BY avg_score DESC puts top performers first.
questions.solution: SELECT category, ROUND(AVG(score), 2) AS avg_score FROM quiz_results GROUP BY category ORDER BY avg_score DESC;

questions.question: Write a SQL query to find all students from the `students` table who have NOT made any submission in the `submissions` table. Return `student_name`, ordered alphabetically.
questions.explanation: Use a LEFT JOIN from students to submissions on student_id. WHERE submissions.id IS NULL filters to students with no matching rows in submissions (i.e., no submissions). This is the standard pattern for anti-joins.
questions.solution: SELECT s.student_name FROM students s LEFT JOIN submissions sub ON s.id = sub.student_id WHERE sub.id IS NULL ORDER BY s.student_name;

questions.question: Write a SQL query to find the student with the highest score in each category. Return `category`, `student`, and `score`, ordered by `category` ascending.
questions.explanation: Use a subquery or CTE to find MAX(score) per category. Then JOIN back to the original table to fetch the student name for that max score. INNER JOIN on both category and max_score ensures correct matching.
questions.solution: SELECT q.category, q.student, q.score FROM quiz_results q INNER JOIN (SELECT category, MAX(score) AS max_score FROM quiz_results GROUP BY category) m ON q.category = m.category AND q.score = m.max_score ORDER BY q.category;
